title: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System
authors: Joseph Loscalzo, John F. Keaney, Jr., Calum A. MacRae

items.subtopic: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
items.title: Embryonic Origins and Congenital Heart Disease Correlation
items.content: The heart's development involves multiple progenitor cells from different embryonic origins, which contribute to distinct regions. These origins correlate with specific gene expression profiles that persist into adulthood, explaining why certain congenital and adult cardiac diseases affect particular areas of the heart.
keyPoints: Early cardiac progenitors arise in lateral splanchnic mesoderm.
Distinctive gene expressions (e.g., Islet-1) contribute to specific heart regions like the right atrium and ventricle.
Embryonic origins correlate with single-cell RNA sequencing profiles decades later, explaining regional susceptibility to diseases.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM

items.subtopic: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
items.title: Role of Neural Crest Cells in Great Vessel Formation
items.content: Neural crest cells are crucial for remodeling and septation during the development of the aortic arch arteries. Deficiencies in vitamin A or folic acid can lead to congenital heart diseases due to abnormal remodeling.
keyPoints: Neural crest cells orchestrate aortic arch remodeling and truncus arteriosus septation.
Smooth muscle in certain vessels derives from neural crest cells.
Maternal deficiencies of vitamin A or folic acid are linked to congenital heart diseases involving the aortic arch.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM

items.subtopic: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
items.title: Development and Disorders of the Cardiac Conduction System
items.content: The cardiac conduction system differentiates from cardiomyocyte precursors, forming both slow-conducting and fast-conducting components essential for proper heart rhythm. Developmental defects can lead to electrophysiologic disorders such as congenital heart block or Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome.
keyPoints: The conduction system includes the SA node, AV node, His bundle, bundle branches, and Purkinje fibers.
SA node precursors originate from the sinus venosus; AV node from the AV canal.
Developmental defects can cause congenital heart block or pre-excitation syndromes.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM

items.title: Diverse Origins of Smooth-Muscle Cells in Arteries
items.content: Smooth-muscle cells in arteries have varied origins depending on their location. Upper-body arterial smooth-muscle cells derive from the neural crest, while lower-body arteries develop these cells from neighboring mesodermal structures.
keyPoints: Upper-body arterial smooth-muscle cells originate from the neural crest.
Lower-body arterial smooth-muscle cells originate from mesodermal structures.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.title: Structural Differences Between Arteries and Veins
items.content: Arteries have a trilaminar structure consisting of the intima, tunica media, and adventitia. The tunica media in arteries is composed of a syncytium of smooth-muscle cells, which are more abundant than in veins.
keyPoints: Arteries have a trilaminar structure: intima, tunica media, and adventitia.
The tunica media in arteries contains a dense layer of smooth-muscle cells.
Veins have sparser smooth-muscle layers compared to arteries.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.title: Endothelial Dysfunction in Vascular Diseases
items.content: The endothelium regulates the passage of molecules and cells, vascular tone, and blood flow. In diseases like atherosclerosis and hypertension, this function is impaired due to reduced production or excess catabolism of homeostatic substances such as nitric oxide.
keyPoints: Endothelial dysfunction leads to capillary leak in conditions like sepsis and pulmonary edema.
Flow-mediated dilatation assesses endothelial vasodilator function.
Impaired production or excess catabolism of NO can cause dysfunctional endothelial properties.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Endothelium's Role in Thrombosis and Hemostasis
items.content: The endothelium regulates thrombosis and hemostasis through various pathways. Inflammatory stimuli can lead to increased production of plasminogen activator inhibitor 1 (PAI-1) and tissue factor, promoting thrombus formation.
keyPoints: Inflammation induces PAI-1 production, inhibiting fibrinolysis.
Tissue factor expression contributes to disseminated intravascular coagulation in sepsis.
Endothelial dysfunction promotes local thrombus accumulation.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Smooth-Muscle Cell Dynamics in Vascular Pathology
items.content: Vascular smooth-muscle cells regulate blood pressure and arterial stiffness. In response to injury or inflammation, they proliferate and migrate, contributing to conditions like atherosclerosis and hypertension.
keyPoints: Proliferation and migration of smooth-muscle cells lead to arterial stenoses in atherosclerosis.
Excessive collagen production by smooth-muscle cells contributes to arterial remodeling.
Arterial stiffness increases left ventricular afterload, indicating poor prognosis.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms of Vasoconstriction and Vasorelaxation
items.content: Vasoconstricting agonists increase intracellular calcium levels through receptor-dependent phospholipase C activation, leading to the production of DAG and IP3. These compounds activate protein kinase C and promote calcium release from the sarco-plasmic reticulum, enhancing contraction. Conversely, vascular smooth-muscle cell tone is modulated by neurotransmitters like norepinephrine, epinephrine, acetylcholine, NO, and ATP through specific receptors that influence intracellular calcium levels.
keyPoints: Vasoconstriction involves receptor-dependent phospholipase C activation producing DAG and IP3.
IP3 increases calcium efflux from the sarco-plasmic reticulum into the cytoplasm.
Norepinephrine activates α-adrenergic receptors causing vasoconstriction in large arteries (α1) and small arterioles (α2).
Epinephrine can activate both α and β adrenergic receptors, with most blood vessels expressing β2-adrenergic receptors leading to relaxation.
Acetylcholine can cause either vasoconstriction or NO-dependent vasorelaxation depending on the receptor type (muscarinic on smooth muscle vs. endothelial cells).
NO released from nitrogenic neurons relaxes vascular smooth-muscle via cyclic GMP-dependent and -independent mechanisms.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Regulation of Vascular Smooth-Muscle Cell Tone
items.content: The autonomic nervous system, through sympathetic, parasympathetic, and nonadrenergic/noncholinergic pathways, modulates vascular smooth-muscle tone. Sympathetic neurons use norepinephrine and epinephrine to activate α-adrenergic receptors for vasoconstriction or β2-adrenergic receptors for relaxation. Parasympathetic neurotransmission involves acetylcholine binding to muscarinic receptors, influencing either contraction or NO-mediated relaxation. Nonadrenergic/noncholinergic pathways include nitrogenic neurons releasing NO and peptidergic inputs that regulate vascular tone.
keyPoints: Sympathetic nervous system uses norepinephrine (α1-adrenergic receptors) and epinephrine (α and β receptors) to modulate vasoconstriction or relaxation.
Parasympathetic neurotransmission involves acetylcholine acting on muscarinic receptors, which can cause vasoconstriction or NO-dependent vasorelaxation.
Nitrogenic neurons release NO, leading to smooth-muscle cell relaxation through cyclic GMP pathways.
Peptidergic inputs (e.g., substance P, VIP) and ATP modulate vascular tone via specific receptors on smooth muscle cells.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Mechanisms of Vascular Growth in Response to Ischemia
items.content: Arteriogenesis and angiogenesis are processes that occur in response to conditions like chronic hypoxemia and tissue ischemia. These involve the recruitment and growth of blood vessels through signaling cascades activated by growth factors such as VEGF and FGF, which stimulate endothelial proliferation and tube formation.
keyPoints: Arteriogenesis involves the expansion of preexisting collateral vascular networks in response to a blocked artery.
Angiogenesis is defined as the stimulation of endothelial proliferation and tube formation.
Growth factors like VEGF and FGF play crucial roles in these processes.
True vascular regeneration, including all three cell layers, does not normally occur in adult mammals.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ARTERIOGENESIS AND ANGIOGENESIS | CARDIAC ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.title: Characteristics of Cardiomyocytes and Sarcomeres
items.content: Cardiomyocytes make up most of the ventricular mass, with dimensions typically ranging from 60–140 µm in length and 17–25 µm in diameter. Each cardiomyocyte contains myofibrils composed of sarcomeres, which are the structural units of contraction.
keyPoints: Cardiomyocytes contain multiple myofibrils running their length, with a centrally located nucleus, mitochondria, and SR.
Sarcomeres lie between Z lines, measuring 1.6 to 2.2 µm in distance depending on muscle stretch.
The A band within the sarcomere is dark and constant at 1.5 µm, flanked by variable-length I bands.
Thick myosin filaments (10 nm) traverse the A band, while thinner actin filaments (5 nm) extend from Z lines through the I band into the A band.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ARTERIOGENESIS AND ANGIOGENESIS | CARDIAC ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.title: Mechanism of Muscle Contraction: Sliding Filament Model
items.content: The sliding filament model explains muscle contraction by describing how actin and myosin filaments interact. During activation, actin filaments slide into the A band while the length of the A band remains constant. The I band shortens and Z lines move closer together.
keyPoints: A band length is constant during contraction and relaxation.
I band shortens as actin slides further into the A band.
Z lines move toward each other during contraction.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · THE CONTRACTILE PROCESS

items.title: Role of Calcium in Muscle Contraction
items.content: Calcium ions ($Ca^{2+}$) play a crucial role in muscle contraction by binding to troponin C, which induces conformational changes that expose actin cross-bridge interaction sites. This process is essential for the cyclic interactions between myosin heads and actin filaments.
keyPoints: $Ca^{2+}$ binds to troponin C during cardiac myocyte activation.
Conformational change in tropomyosin exposes actin binding sites.
Cross-bridge cycling ceases when $[Ca^{2+}]$ falls below a critical level.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · THE CONTRACTILE PROCESS

items.title: Inotropic State and Positive Inotropes
items.content: The inotropic state of the heart is primarily determined by cytoplasmic $Ca^{2+}$ levels. Agents like digitalis glycosides and $\beta$-adrenergic agonists increase cytoplasmic $Ca^{2+}$, enhancing cross-bridge cycling and myocardial contractility.
keyPoints: Cytoplasmic $[Ca^{2+}]$ is a principal determinant of the heart's inotropic state.
Positive inotropes increase cytoplasmic $[Ca^{2+}]$, stimulating contraction.
$\beta$-adrenergic agonists enhance $Ca^{2+}$ influx via PKA-mediated phosphorylation.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · THE CONTRACTILE PROCESS

items.title: Understanding Cardiac Action Potential Phases and Their Role in Contraction
items.content: The cardiac action potential comprises four phases, crucial for the regulation of myocardial contraction. Phase 2, known as the plateau phase, is characterized by a slow inward current through L-type Ca²⁺ channels. This influx triggers further Ca²⁺ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) via Ca²⁺-induced Ca²⁺ release, which is essential for muscle contraction.
keyPoints: Resting transmembrane potential: -80 to -100 mV
Phase 2 involves L-type Ca²⁺ channels and a slow inward current
Ca²⁺-induced Ca²⁺ release from SR triggers contraction
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC ACTIVATION

items.title: Role of Calcium Handling in Cardiac Contraction and Relaxation
items.content: Calcium handling is central to cardiac muscle function. During repolarization, Ca²⁺ uptake by the SR via SERCA₂ₐ reduces cytoplasmic [Ca²⁺], facilitating myocardial relaxation. This process is ATP-dependent and involves calsequestrin for Ca²⁺ storage in the SR. PKA phosphorylation of phospholamban enhances SERCA₂ₐ activity, optimizing calcium reuptake and accelerating relaxation.
keyPoints: SERCA₂ₐ mediates Ca²⁺ uptake into SR during repolarization
Ca²⁺ uptake is ATP-dependent and involves calsequestrin
PKA phosphorylation of phospholamban enhances SERCA₂ₐ activity
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC ACTIVATION

items.subtopic: CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE
items.title: Optimal Preload and Sarcomere Length for Cardiac Contractility
items.content: Preload, defined as the length of muscle at the onset of contraction, is crucial in determining cardiac contractile force. Optimal contractile force occurs when sarcomeres are approximately 2.2 µm long, maximizing $ Ca^{2+} $ sensitivity and myofilament interactions.
keyPoints: Preload determines sarcomere length at the onset of contraction.
Contractile force is optimal at a sarcomere length of ~2.2 µm.
Starling's law: Ventricular contraction force depends on end-diastolic muscle length, closely related to ventricular end-diastolic volume.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE

items.subtopic: CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE
items.title: Impact of Preload and Afterload on Stroke Volume and Cardiac Contractility
items.content: Stroke volume is directly related to end-diastolic fiber length (preload) and inversely related to arterial resistance (afterload). In heart failure, despite normal or elevated end-diastolic volumes, reduced contractility leads to decreased stroke volume.
keyPoints: Stroke volume varies directly with preload and inversely with afterload.
In heart failure, reduced contractility results in smaller stroke volume from a normal/elevated end-diastolic volume.
Ventricular function curve: An upward/left shift indicates increased contractility; downward/right shift indicates decreased contractility.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE

items.title: How Afterload Affects Myocardial Fiber Shortening and Stroke Volume
items.content: Afterload, defined as the tension opposing ventricular shortening during ejection, is influenced by aortic pressure, ventricular volume, and myocardial wall thickness. According to Laplace's law, increased afterload reduces myocardial fiber shortening, thereby decreasing stroke volume. Elevated arterial pressure from vasoconstriction increases afterload, which further opposes fiber shortening.
keyPoints: Afterload is the tension opposing ventricular muscle fiber shortening during ejection.
Determined by aortic pressure, ventricular cavity volume, and myocardial wall thickness.
Laplace's law: Tension = (Intra-cavitary pressure × Ventricular radius) / Wall thickness.
Increased afterload reduces myocardial fiber shortening and stroke volume.
Elevated arterial pressure from vasoconstriction increases afterload.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VENTRICULAR AFTERLOAD

items.title: Impact of Afterload on Cardiac Output in Impaired Contractility
items.content: In cases where myocardial contractility is impaired, ventricular dilation occurs, increasing afterload as per Laplace's law. This rise in afterload limits cardiac output. Increased afterload from neural and humoral responses to reduced cardiac output can further decrease it, creating a vicious cycle, particularly in ischemic heart disease. Vasodilators reduce afterload, thereby enhancing cardiac output.
keyPoints: Impaired myocardial contractility leads to ventricular dilation and increased afterload.
Increased afterload limits cardiac output.
Neural and humoral responses can further increase afterload in response to reduced cardiac output.
Vicious cycle of increasing afterload and decreasing cardiac output, especially in ischemic heart disease.
Vasodilators reduce afterload, improving cardiac output.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VENTRICULAR AFTERLOAD

items.subtopic: TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION
items.title: Exercise and its Impact on Cardiac Function
items.content: During exercise, the body's response involves increased venous return due to hyperventilation, muscle pumping action, and venoconstriction. This enhances preload while adrenergic stimulation and tachycardia boost myocardial contractility. Vasodilation in active muscles limits afterload increase, allowing high cardiac output with minimal rise in arterial pressure.
keyPoints: Hyperventilation, muscle pumping, and venoconstriction augment venous return and preload during exercise.
Adrenergic stimulation and tachycardia elevate myocardial contractility.
Vasodilation in exercising muscles limits afterload increase despite high cardiac output.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION
items.title: Ejection Fraction as a Sensitive Index of Cardiac Function
items.content: The ejection fraction, defined as the ratio of stroke volume to end-diastolic volume (normal value = 67 ± 8%), is a sensitive measure for detecting systolic heart failure. It can be depressed even when stroke volume appears normal, providing insight into cardiac function beyond basic output measures.
keyPoints: Ejection fraction is the ratio of stroke volume to end-diastolic volume (normal value = 67 ± 8%).
It serves as a sensitive index for systolic heart failure detection.
Depressed ejection fraction can indicate cardiac dysfunction even with normal stroke volume.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION
items.title: Assessment Techniques for Cardiac Function
items.content: Noninvasive techniques like echocardiography, radionuclide scintigraphy, and cardiac MRI are crucial in assessing myocardial function. They measure end-diastolic and end-systolic volumes, ejection fraction, systolic shortening rate, and allow evaluation of ventricular filling and regional contraction. These methods are particularly valuable for diagnosing ischemic heart disease.
keyPoints: Echocardiography, radionuclide scintigraphy, and cardiac MRI assess myocardial function noninvasively.
Measurements include end-diastolic and end-systolic volumes, ejection fraction, and systolic shortening rate.
These techniques are valuable for diagnosing ischemic heart disease due to regional myocardial damage.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR
items.title: Assessing Diastolic Function with Doppler Ultrasound
items.content: Ventricular filling is influenced by myocardial relaxation and passive stiffness. Doppler ultrasound measures flow velocity across the mitral valve, revealing diastolic function. Early diastole normally shows rapid inflow, but impaired relaxation alters this pattern.
keyPoints: Early diastolic filling becomes slower with mild to moderate impairment in relaxation.
Presystolic filling rates rise as early diastolic filling declines.
With further stiffening, flow appears 'pseudo-normalized' due to increased left atrial pressure.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR

items.subtopic: DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR
items.title: Energy Metabolism in the Heart and Its Regulation
items.content: The heart's ATP production is crucial for contraction and homeostasis, with energy demands influenced by tension, frequency of contraction, and contractility. The primary sources are FFAs and glucose, metabolized differently to produce acetyl-CoA.
keyPoints: Heart requires ~15% of the organism's total energy.
FFAs provide ~70% of acetyl-CoA in a fasted state; glucose oxidation increases with feeding.
Increased cardiac work or hypoxia enhances myocardial glucose uptake and metabolism.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR

items.subtopic: DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR
items.title: Potential for Cardiac Regeneration and Repair
items.content: Adult mammalian myocardial cells have limited regenerative potential, but recent advances suggest possibilities for enhancing cardiac repair. CAR-T cell therapy shows promise in reversing fibrosis.
keyPoints: Immature hearts exhibit some regenerative capacity that diminishes with maturity.
Current research focuses on releasing constraints to enhance regeneration post-injury.
CAR-T cells may reverse heart fibrosis, offering potential for reconstructing damaged ventricles.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR

items.subtopic: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
items.question: Which embryonic structure contributes smooth muscle to the coronary arteries?
items.explanation: The proepicardial organ contributes smooth muscle to the coronary arteries.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM

items.subtopic: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
items.question: What is required for proper aortic arch remodeling and septation?
items.explanation: Neural crest cells are necessary for aortic arch remodeling and septation.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM

items.subtopic: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
items.question: Which vitamin deficiencies can lead to congenital heart diseases involving abnormal remodeling of the aortic arch arteries?
items.explanation: Maternal deficiencies of vitamin A and folic acid are associated with congenital heart diseases involving abnormal aortic arch remodeling.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM

items.subtopic: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
items.question: Which cells undergo an epithelial-mesenchymal transformation to form mature valve leaflets?
items.explanation: Underlying endocardial cells undergo epithelial-mesenchymal transformation to form mature valve leaflets.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM

items.subtopic: ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE
items.question: Which embryonic origin do endothelial progenitor cells derive from?
items.explanation: Embryonic endothelial progenitor cells are derived from mesoderm.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.subtopic: ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE
items.question: From which origin do some upper-body arterial smooth-muscle cells derive?
items.explanation: Some upper-body arterial smooth-muscle cells derive from the neural crest.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.subtopic: ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE
items.question: What is the role of bone marrow clonality in aging arteries?
options: It imparts significant clonality to endothelial cell populations.
It reduces vascular stem cell activity.
items.explanation: Bone marrow clonality, increasingly prevalent in aging, may impart significant clonality to endothelial cell populations.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.question: Which substance produced by endothelial cells provides tonic stimulation of endothelial homeostatic properties under physiological conditions?
items.explanation: Endogenous substances like nitric oxide provide tonic stimulation for endothelial homeostasis.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: What can excessive production of reactive oxygen species by endothelial cells lead to?
options: Local oxidative stress and inactivation of NO
items.explanation: Excessive reactive oxygen species can promote local oxidative stress, leading to the inactivation of nitric oxide.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: What is a major homeostatic influence on endothelial function?
items.explanation: Laminar blood flow significantly influences endothelial vasodilator function.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: What do endothelial cells produce to inhibit smooth-muscle proliferation?
items.explanation: Heparan sulfate glycosaminoglycans produced by endothelial cells inhibit smooth-muscle proliferation.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a membrane lipid derivative that activates protein kinase C and increases intracellular $[Ca^{2+}]$?
items.explanation: Diacylglycerol (DAG) is a membrane lipid derivative that activates protein kinase C and increases intracellular $[Ca^{2+}]$.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which neurotransmitter released from parasympathetic neurons can cause NO-dependent vasorelaxation by binding to endothelial cells?
items.explanation: Acetylcholine released from parasympathetic neurons can cause NO-dependent vasorelaxation by binding to endothelial cells.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which adrenergic receptor subtype is primarily activated by norepinephrine in small arteries and arterioles, leading to vasoconstriction?
items.explanation: Norepinephrine activates $\alpha_2$ receptors in small arteries and arterioles, leading to vasoconstriction.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which growth factor is involved in stimulating endothelial proliferation and tube formation during angiogenesis?
items.explanation: VEGF is a key growth factor that stimulates endothelial proliferation and tube formation in angiogenesis.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ARTERIOGENESIS AND ANGIOGENESIS | CARDIAC ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.question: What does arteriogenesis refer to?
options: Recruitment and expansion of preexisting collateral vascular networks
True vascular regeneration in adult mammals
Development of a single-layered blood vessel
items.explanation: Arteriogenesis involves the recruitment and expansion of preexisting collateral vascular networks.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ARTERIOGENESIS AND ANGIOGENESIS | CARDIAC ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.question: Which statement is true regarding true vascular regeneration in adult mammals?
options: It involves all three cell layers of a new blood vessel
It does not normally occur, but advances may change this
It only involves the development of capillaries
items.explanation: True vascular regeneration normally does not occur in adult mammals, though scientific advances might help.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ARTERIOGENESIS AND ANGIOGENESIS | CARDIAC ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.question: Which protein complex is responsible for regulating actin-myosin interactions in muscle contraction?
items.explanation: The troponin complex (C, I, and T) regulates actin-myosin interactions by undergoing conformational changes in response to calcium binding.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · THE CONTRACTILE PROCESS

items.question: What is the primary role of myosin ATPase during muscle contraction?
options: To break down ATP and supply energy for contraction
To stabilize tropomyosin on actin filaments
items.explanation: Myosin ATPase breaks down ATP to provide the energy necessary for cross-bridge cycling and muscle contraction.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · THE CONTRACTILE PROCESS

items.question: What happens to the I band during muscle contraction?
items.explanation: During muscle contraction, the I band shortens as actin filaments slide further into the A band.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · THE CONTRACTILE PROCESS

items.question: Which protein is responsible for tethering the sarcomere to the cell membrane?
items.explanation: Dystrophin tethers the sarcomere to the cell membrane by binding to the dystroglycan complex at membrane adherens junctions.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · THE CONTRACTILE PROCESS

items.question: Which ion's influx through L-type Ca²⁺ channels is responsible for triggering substantial Ca²⁺ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) during phase 2 of the cardiac action potential?
items.explanation: The slow inward current through L-type Ca²⁺ channels during phase 2 of the action potential triggers further Ca²⁺ release from the SR.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC ACTIVATION

items.question: What is the primary function of SERCA₂ₐ in cardiac muscle cells?
options: Uptaking Ca²⁺ into the sarcoplasmic reticulum
Releasing Ca²⁺ from the sarcoplasmic reticulum
items.explanation: SERCA₂ₐ is responsible for ATP-dependent uptake of Ca²⁺ into the SR, which lowers cytoplasmic [Ca²⁺] and promotes myocardial relaxation.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC ACTIVATION

items.question: Which protein's phosphorylation by PKA enhances SERCA₂ₐ activity in cardiac muscle cells?
items.explanation: PKA phosphorylates phospholamban, which enhances SERCA₂ₐ activity and increases SR Ca²⁺ uptake.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC ACTIVATION

items.subtopic: CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE
items.question: Which factor primarily determines the sarcomere length at the onset of contraction?
items.explanation: Preload determines sarcomere length at the onset of contraction.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE

items.subtopic: CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE
items.question: According to Starling's law, what does ventricular contraction force depend on within physiological limits?
items.explanation: Starling's law states that ventricular contraction force depends on the end-diastolic length of the cardiac muscle.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE

items.subtopic: CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE
items.question: What does an increase in contractility cause in the ventricular function curve?
options: Shift downward and to the right
Shift upward and to the left
Shift downward and to the left
items.explanation: An increase in contractility shifts the ventricular function curve upward and to the left.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE

items.question: Which factor does NOT directly determine ventricular afterload according to Laplace's law?
items.explanation: Laplace's law states that ventricular afterload is determined by aortic pressure, ventricular radius, and wall thickness, not myocardial contractility.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VENTRICULAR AFTERLOAD

items.question: What happens to the afterload on a dilated left ventricle compared to a normalized one at any given aortic pressure?
items.explanation: At any given aortic pressure, the afterload on a dilated left ventricle exceeds that on a normalized ventricle.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VENTRICULAR AFTERLOAD

items.question: How does an increase in arterial pressure affect stroke volume?
options: Increases it by augmenting myocardial fiber shortening
Decreases it by opposing myocardial fiber shortening
Has no effect on stroke volume
items.explanation: An increase in arterial pressure augments afterload, which opposes myocardial fiber shortening and reduces stroke volume.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VENTRICULAR AFTERLOAD

items.subtopic: TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION
items.question: Which factor is NOT a determinant of myocardial contractility during exercise?
items.explanation: Vasoconstriction in exercising muscles affects venous return and preload, not myocardial contractility.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION
items.question: What primarily limits the increase in afterload during exercise?
items.explanation: Vasodilation occurs in the exercising muscles, limiting the increase in afterload.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION
items.question: Which index is sensitive to systolic heart failure even when stroke volume is normal?
items.explanation: The ejection fraction is a sensitive index of cardiac function in systolic heart failure.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION
items.question: Which noninvasive technique is NOT mentioned for assessing myocardial function?
items.explanation: Electrocardiogram is not mentioned as a noninvasive technique for assessing myocardial function.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR
items.question: Which factor is primarily responsible for the rapid early diastolic inflow velocity across the mitral valve in a healthy heart?
options: Reduced passive stiffness of the ventricular wall
items.explanation: In a healthy heart, rapid early diastolic inflow velocity is largely due to enhanced myocardial relaxation.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR

items.subtopic: DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR
items.question: What condition can lead to pseudo-normalization of early ventricular filling?
options: Mildly impaired relaxation with rising left atrial pressure
Increased ATP production in the heart
Decreased passive stiffness of the ventricular wall
items.explanation: Pseudo-normalization occurs when early ventricular filling becomes more rapid due to rising left atrial pressure, often seen with further stiffening.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR

items.subtopic: CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR
items.question: Which metabolic substrate primarily supplies the heart's acetyl-CoA in the fasted state?
items.explanation: In the fasted, resting state, circulating free fatty acids furnish most of the heart's acetyl-CoA (~70%).
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR

items.subtopic: CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR
items.question: What is a consequence of severe myocardial ischemia on glucose metabolism?
options: Production of lactic acid via anaerobic glycolysis
items.explanation: Severe myocardial ischemia inhibits cytoplasmic pyruvate dehydrogenase, leading to incomplete glucose metabolism and the production of lactic acid via anaerobic glycolysis.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR

items.subtopic: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
items.statement: The sinoatrial (SA) node arises from precursors within the AV canal.
items.explanation: The SA node arises from precursors within the sinus venosus, not the AV canal.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM

items.subtopic: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
items.statement: Coronary artery formation does not require additional cell populations.
items.explanation: Epicardial cells from the proepicardial organ are required for proper coronary patterning.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM

items.subtopic: ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE
items.statement: Vascular stem cells resident in the vessel wall can give rise to smooth-muscle cells in injured arteries.
items.explanation: Vascular stem cells resident in the vessel wall may give rise to some smooth-muscle cells in injured or atheromatous arteries.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.subtopic: ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE
items.statement: The tunica media of veins has more smooth-muscle cells than that of arteries.
items.explanation: In veins, the smooth-muscle cells in the tunica media are much sparser than in arteries.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.statement: Endothelial dysfunction tends to promote local thrombus accumulation in pathologic circumstances.
items.explanation: In pathological conditions, endothelial dysfunction promotes thrombosis rather than preventing it.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: Smooth-muscle cells in adult vessels frequently replicate without arterial injury or inflammatory activation.
items.explanation: Smooth-muscle cells seldom replicate unless there is arterial injury or inflammation.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: Calcium increases myosin light chain kinase activity via calmodulin, enhancing vascular smooth-muscle contraction.
items.explanation: Calcium activates myosin light chain kinase through calmodulin, which enhances contraction.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: Epinephrine primarily causes vasoconstriction in most blood vessels by activating $\alpha$-adrenergic receptors.
items.explanation: In most blood vessels, epinephrine activates both $\alpha$ and $\beta$ receptors; $\beta_2$-adrenergic receptor activation leads to relaxation.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: The sarcomere is the structural and functional unit of contraction in cardiomyocytes.
items.explanation: The sarcomere lies between adjacent Z lines and is the fundamental unit of muscle contraction.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ARTERIOGENESIS AND ANGIOGENESIS | CARDIAC ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.statement: Thick filaments in cardiomyocytes are composed primarily of actin.
items.explanation: Thick filaments are composed principally of myosin, not actin.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ARTERIOGENESIS AND ANGIOGENESIS | CARDIAC ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.statement: The A band shortens during muscle contraction.
items.explanation: The A band remains constant in length during both contraction and relaxation.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · THE CONTRACTILE PROCESS

items.statement: Increased cytoplasmic Ca2+ concentration is a principal determinant of the heart's inotropic state.
items.explanation: Cytoplasmic [Ca2+] determines myocardial contractility and influences cross-bridge cycling.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · THE CONTRACTILE PROCESS

items.statement: The resting membrane potential of a cardiomyocyte is approximately -80 to -100 mV.
items.explanation: In the inactive state, the cardiomyocyte membrane is electrically polarized with a transmembrane potential of -80 to -100 mV.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC ACTIVATION

items.statement: The primary source of Ca²⁺ for cardiac muscle contraction is extracellular.
items.explanation: While initial Ca²⁺ influx triggers SR release, the primary source of Ca²⁺ for contraction comes from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CARDIAC ACTIVATION

items.subtopic: CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE
items.statement: Ventricular end-diastolic pressure is unrelated to ventricular stroke volume.
items.explanation: Ventricular end-diastolic pressure can serve as a surrogate for end-diastolic volume, which influences stroke volume.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE

items.subtopic: CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE
items.statement: As heart failure progresses and contractility declines, the heart delivers a larger stroke volume from an elevated end-diastolic volume.
items.explanation: As heart fails and contractility declines, it delivers a progressively smaller stroke volume even from normal or elevated end-diastolic volumes.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE

items.statement: Increased afterload can initiate a vicious circle by further reducing cardiac output.
items.explanation: Increased afterload, especially in patients with ischemic heart disease, may reduce cardiac output further, increasing ventricular volume and initiating a vicious circle.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VENTRICULAR AFTERLOAD

items.statement: Vasodilators increase afterload and decrease cardiac output.
items.explanation: Treatment with vasodilators reduces afterload, which in turn increases cardiac output.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VENTRICULAR AFTERLOAD

items.subtopic: TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION
items.statement: The end-systolic left ventricular pressure-volume relationship is influenced by preload and afterload.
items.explanation: This relationship does not depend on preload and afterload.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION
items.statement: Echocardiography can measure ejection fraction.
items.explanation: Echocardiography is used to assess myocardial function, including ejection fraction.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR
items.statement: In the fed state, myocardial glucose uptake and metabolism to pyruvate decrease.
items.explanation: In the fed state, with elevations of blood glucose and insulin, glucose oxidation increases.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR

items.subtopic: CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR
items.statement: Adult mammalian myocardial cells have significant regenerative potential.
items.explanation: Adult mammalian myocardial cells are fully differentiated and have little or no regenerative potential.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR

items.subtopic: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
items.assertion: The AV node reduces transmission of higher impulse rates to the ventricles.
items.reason: This delay enables sequential antegrade contraction between atria and ventricles.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM

items.subtopic: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
items.assertion: The conduction system consists solely of cardiomyocyte precursors.
items.reason: Precursors within the sinus venosus give rise to the SA node.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the conduction system also includes heterogeneous cell populations, not just cardiomyocyte precursors.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System | DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY OF THE CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM

items.subtopic: ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE
items.reason: The intima consists of a monolayer of endothelial cells, the tunica media is made up of smooth-muscle cells, and the adventitia contains extracellular matrix with fibroblasts.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ☑ ORIGIN OF VASCULAR CELLS | VASCULAR ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.assertion: Endothelial cells regulate the balance between thrombosis and hemostasis.
items.reason: Inflammatory cytokines can induce endothelial cells to produce plasminogen activator inhibitor 1, a major inhibitor of fibrinolysis.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; the reason explains how inflammatory stimuli affect thrombosis and hemostasis.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Smooth-muscle cells contribute to arterial stenoses in atherosclerosis.
items.reason: Smooth-muscle cell proliferation is induced by endothelium-derived growth factors and chemoattractants like platelet-derived growth factor.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason explaining the role of growth factors in smooth-muscle proliferation.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Nitric oxide causes vasorelaxation in vascular smooth-muscle cells.
items.reason: Nitric oxide activates cyclic GMP-dependent and -independent mechanisms to induce relaxation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · VASCULAR CELL BIOLOGY (part 2)

items.assertion: The A band in the sarcomere has a constant length.
items.reason: The A band is flanked by two lighter bands, the I bands, which are of variable length.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains why the A band's length remains constant.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · ARTERIOGENESIS AND ANGIOGENESIS | CARDIAC ULTRASTRUCTURE

items.assertion: Calcium binding to troponin C leads to muscle contraction.
items.reason: This causes conformational changes in tropomyosin, exposing actin cross-bridge interaction sites.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why calcium binding leads to muscle contraction.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · THE CONTRACTILE PROCESS

items.assertion: The SR network is unrelated to the T system in cardiac myocytes.
items.reason: The transverse tubules arise as sarcolemmal invaginations that extend into the myofibrils along the Z lines.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the SR and T system are closely related both structurally and functionally.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · THE CONTRACTILE PROCESS

items.subtopic: CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE
items.assertion: An increase in preload results in an increased stroke volume.
items.reason: Preload determines sarcomere length at the onset of contraction, optimizing contractile force.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why an increase in preload increases stroke volume.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · CONTROL OF CARDIAC PERFORMANCE AND OUTPUT | THE ROLE OF MUSCLE LENGTH (PRELOAD) | CARDIAC PERFORMANCE

items.subtopic: TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION
items.assertion: Ventricular end-diastolic volume is a normal value for assessing left ventricular systolic impairment.
items.reason: Abnormally elevated ventricular end-diastolic volume signifies left ventricular systolic impairment.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION
items.assertion: Ejection fraction is always a reliable measure of cardiac function.
items.reason: Strong dependence on ventricular loading conditions influences the precision of ejection fraction measurements.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; while ejection fraction is useful, its reliability can be influenced by loading conditions.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · TABLE 244-2 Determinants of Stroke Volume | III. Myocardial Contractility | EXERCISE | ASSESSMENT OF CARDIAC FUNCTION

items.subtopic: DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR
items.assertion: Increased passive stiffness of the ventricular wall can result from hypertrophy and fibrosis.
items.reason: Ventricular stiffness increases with conditions like hypertrophy, fibrosis, or pericardial constriction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR

items.subtopic: CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR
items.assertion: High concentrations of circulating FFAs improve oxidative phosphorylation.
items.reason: FFA breakdown products may exert toxic or arrhythmogenic effects on cardiac cell membranes.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because high concentrations of circulating FFAs reduce oxidative phosphorylation, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Basic Biology of the Cardiovascular System · DIASTOLIC FUNCTION | CARDIAC METABOLISM | CARDIAC CELLULAR INTERVENTIONS: REGENERATION AND REPAIR
